prompt: Explain in two short sentences why CPU interference can increase inference latency on an embedded board.
reference_for_bertscore: CPU interference can increase inference latency because the model shares cores, caches and memory bandwidth with other processes. This contention delays prompt processing and token generation.

prompt: Explain the difference between throughput and latency in AI inference using one short paragraph.
reference_for_bertscore: Latency measures how long one inference takes from input to output, while throughput measures how many tokens or requests are processed per unit of time. A system can have high throughput but still be unsuitable for real-time use if individual responses arrive too late.
output: Throughput refers to the amount of data processed or information processed per unit of time. Latency, on the other hand, indicates the time taken to process data or information. In AI inference, throughput is crucial for managing the load on the system and ensuring that the system can efficiently process data or information. The goal is to maximize throughput without compromising on latency, as high latency can lead to performance degradation.

prompt: Explain the difference between throughput and latency in AI inference using one short paragraph.
reference_for_bertscore: Latency measures how long one inference takes from input to output, while throughput measures how many tokens or requests are processed per unit of time. A system can have high throughput but still be unsuitable for real-time use if individual responses arrive too late.
output: Throughput refers to the number of predictions or inference operations that can be performed per unit of time. It measures the speed at which AI models process data. Latency, on the other hand, refers to the time it takes for a prediction or inference operation to complete. It includes both the time it takes to start the operation and the time it takes to complete the operation. In AI inference, throughput is crucial for ensuring that the model can handle a large volume of data efficiently and quickly, while latency is important for the user experience, especially in real-time applications.

prompt: Explain the difference between throughput and latency in AI inference using one short paragraph.
reference_for_bertscore: Latency measures how long one inference takes from input to output, while throughput measures how many tokens or requests are processed per unit of time. A system can have high throughput but still be unsuitable for real-time use if individual responses arrive too late.
output: Throughput refers to the amount of data processed or the number of operations performed in a given time period, typically measured in units such as "per second" or "per minute." In the context of AI inference, it indicates how many tasks or operations are completed within a short period of time, such as in a single inference call. Latency, on the other hand, refers to the delay between the start of an inference task and the completion of the task. It is the time it takes for the processing of data to reach the inference engine, and it can be influenced by factors such as network latency, the size of the data, and the complexity of the model. In AI inference, both throughput and latency are important metrics that need to be considered in the design and optimization of inference systems.
